title: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure and Patch Management Tracking
exclusions: Does not confirm that vulnerability disclosures followed coordinated procedures, verify that patch testing occurred, determine whether the entity's disclosure policy meets ENISA guidance, or replace counsel review. Only tracks patch deployment and CVE remediation timelines.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.3` outputs, weekly dashboard PDF, and tamper-evident archive of result snapshots in `index=audit_evidence` referenced by the control matrix row.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(e) requires vulnerability handling and disclosure. The UC tracks patch deployment cadence and Dependabot/CVE alert remediation times in Splunk but cannot confirm that disclosure procedures were followed or that vendor coordination occurred, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) (Security in acquisition, development and maintenance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.3: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure and Patch Management Tracking.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.3' running on index=vulnerability sourcetype="tenable:vuln" (cve, severity, plugin_name, host, first_found, last_fixed, state), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Seed Tenable-shaped events where `state=Active`, severity is Critical, and `first_found` is older than eight days; the alert fires listing the host with `max_age` above the seven-day SLA and includes open_vulns counts.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest matching events for the same host but include an open exception record in `vuln_exception_register.csv` covering the plugin_id until next quarter; the scheduled search returns no rows after the lookup filter is applied.

dataSources: `index=vulnerability` (or `index=vuln`) `sourcetype=tenable:vuln` fields host, severity, first_found, state from Tenable Add-On for Splunk ([Splunkbase 4060](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4060)); complementary patch posture from `index=patch` `sourcetype=wsus:update` or `sourcetype=sccm:compliance` via Splunk Add-on for Microsoft SCCM ([Splunkbase 1599](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1599)) with BulletinID, ComplianceState — map to CIM Vulnerabilities for board-level rollups.
app: Tenable Add-On for Splunk ([Splunkbase 4060](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4060)); Splunk Add-on for Microsoft SCCM (Splunkbase 1599) when correlating patch deployment; optional CIM Vulneraries data model acceleration on the search head.
spl:
index=vulnerability sourcetype="tenable:vuln" state="Active"
| eval host=coalesce(host, hostname, dns_name)
| eval first_found=coalesce(first_found, first_seen)
| eval age_days=round((now()-first_found)/86400, 1)
| eval sla_days=case(severity="Critical",7, severity="High",30, 1=1,90)
| eval sla_breach=if(age_days>sla_days, 1, 0)
| stats count as open_vulns, max(age_days) as max_age by host, severity
| where sla_breach=1
| sort - max_age
| table host, severity, open_vulns, max_age, sla_days
description: Surfaces active Tenable-plugin findings where the time since first_seen exceeds policy-based remediation deadlines per severity bucket for each host.
value: Compliance teams can hand auditors a repeatable report that shows which servers breached internal fix deadlines and for how long, instead of exporting ad-hoc CSV snapshots from the scanner console every quarter.
implementation: (1) Install Tenable Add-On (4060) and confirm events land in `index=vulnerability` with `state` populated. (2) Open **Settings → Data Summary → Sourcetypes** and verify whether your feed uses `first_seen` vs `first_found`. (3) Copy SLA constants into a macro `nis2_sla_severity(1)` so operations can change thresholds once. (4) For patch proof, mirror SCCM (1599) compliance into `index=patch` and schedule a follow-on correlation search. (5) Route alerts to SecOps on-call with the host CMDB owner field.
visualization: Table of hosts past SLA, bar chart of open_vulns by severity, line chart of rolling count of breached hosts over 90 days.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.severity | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
• **Tenable plugin false positives on legacy middle-ware** — Distinguish: plugin ID flagged for years with formal VA waiver in ServiceNow. Suppress: lookup `vuln_exception_register.csv` with plugin_id, host, expiry_date.
• **Test or scan labs outside production scope** — Distinguish: host matches `lab_prefix` from CMDB. Suppress: `where NOT match(host,"^(lab|tst)-")` or join asset class lookup.
• **Deferred patching inside approved cyber-maintenance blackout** — Distinguish: CAB-approved freeze with CHG record. Suppress: time-bound exception table `patch_blackout_exceptions.csv` keyed on host and epoch window.
• **Field mapping drift (`first_seen` null)** — Distinguish: scanner upgrade renamed fields; age stays zero. Suppress: fix `props.conf` on indexers, interim filter `where isnotnull(first_found)`.
• **Container hosts counted twice** — Distinguish: duplicate assets for docker host vs cluster member. Suppress: dedup on `host` after `rex` normalization to lowercase FQDN.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964, alternative vulnerability scanner)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, change_request for patch tracking)
Splunk Add-on for Tenable (Splunkbase 4060, vulnerability detection data)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Assets & Identities for asset enrichment)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Tenable Add-on for Splunk" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `vulnerability` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(e)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) (Security in acquisition, development and maintenance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.3: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure and Patch Management Tracking." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Tenable Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase app ID 4060), landing events in `vulnerability` under `tenable:vuln`. Daily vulnerability deltas for a 10000-asset estate produce 50000-500000 findings/day (~5-50 GB/month). Full snapshots weekly add more.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > Tenable.sc Vulnerability. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[tenable_sc_vuln://prod]
endpoint = https://tenable.example.com
interval = 86400
index = vulnerability
sourcetype = tenable:sc:vuln
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `pluginID, severity, state, first_seen, last_seen, host, dns, ip, plugin_name, cve`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=vulnerability earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


8 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Install Tenable Add-On. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** and confirm events land in `index=vulnerability` with `state` populated. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Open **Settings → Data Summary → Sourcetypes** and verify whether your feed uses `first_seen` vs `first_found`. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 4:** Copy SLA constants into a macro `nis2_sla_severity. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** ` so operations can change thresholds once. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 6:** For patch proof, mirror SCCM. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 7:** compliance into `index=patch` and schedule a follow-on correlation search. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 8:** Route alerts to SecOps on-call with the host CMDB owner field. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `max()` across the full search range, grouped by `host, severity`. For "NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure and Patch Management Tracking," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `host, severity` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(host) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Vulnerabilities` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.severity | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Vulnerabilities` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.3: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure and Patch Management Tracking]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.3,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=vulnerability sourcetype=tenable:vuln earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `age_days`, `first_found`, `host`, `max_age`, `open_vulns`, `severity`, `sla_breach`, `sla_days`. Run:

```spl
index=vulnerability sourcetype=tenable:vuln earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table age_days, first_found, host, max_age, open_vulns, severity, sla_breach, sla_days
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.3: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Hosts Past Sla (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Open Vulns By Severity (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Line Chart Of Rolling Count Of Breached Hosts Over 90 Days (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We measure how long serious security holes have stayed open on each computer. When something important has waited too long, we raise it so the people responsible for fixing it can act before auditors or criminals force the issue.
